texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A new pressure drop equation has been developed from the continuity equation and the momentum balance equations with the consideration of gas compressibility and " bre permeability. For the case of negligible permeation # ux, the pressure equation reduces to the Hagen } Poiseuille equation. The e ! ects of design variables such as membrane permeability and " bre radius on the pressure pro " les and stage cut obtained from the two pressure models have been discussed. In addition, the e ! ects of using the two pressure models, feed mode and membrane selectivity on operating variables such as the pressure ratio across the membrane and stage cut have also been studied. Numerical solutions of the two pressure models have been obtained for the separation of a volatile organic vapour from N 2 stream for the countercurrent # ow pattern. The use of the Hagen } Poiseuille equation will result in either an overestimation or underestimation of the membrane area required at the stipulated stage cut depending on the feed mode operation. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Separation of gases/vapours using polymeric membranes has become an important unit operation due to the discovery of economically competitive membranes with high permeabilities and good selectivities. This improved energy e $ ciency and ease of operation and scale-up are the main advantages of this technique. Mathematical modelling of this process has attracted considerable attention in the past. Weller and Steiner (1950a,b) performed the " rst analytical study in binary gas separation with a non-porous membrane.

texts.orig: Several investigators (Pan & Habgood, 1974; Blaisdell & Kammermeyer, 1973; Walawender & Stern, 1972; Weller & Steiner, 1950a,b) have attempted the modelling of a gas permeator for separation of a binary gas mixture without considering pressure drop in lumen of the " bres. Later, some models were extended to include the pressure drop inside the " bres (Thorman, Rhim & Stern, 1975; Antonson, Gardener, King & Ko, 1977; Pan & Habgood, 1978; Pan, 1983; Chern, Koros & Fedkiw, 1985; Pettersen & Lien, 1994) using the Hagen } Poiseuille equation which may lead to a deviation when the permeation rate becomes high.

texts.orig: Many theoretical analyses have appeared in the literature describing the relationship between pressure, axial # ow and wall suction for incompressible # uids. Berman (1953,1958) and Yuan and Finkelsten (1956) were the " rst to solve the Navier } Stokes equations for laminar # ow in a porous slit and porous tube, respectively. They and most other workers assumed that the axial # ow was fully developed and that the shape of the non-dimensional velocity pro " le was similar or invariant with axial distance. Terrill (1983) developed equations from the continuity equation and the Navier } Stokes equations to relate axial velocity and axial pressure distribution to wall suction. To understand the in # uence of wall suction on the axial pressure drop in membrane modules, Mellis, Gill and Belfort (1993) developed two analytical solutions of this problem with laminar # ow and conducted experiments to measure the axial pressure drop for liquid # ow in a porous tube. The " rst approach assumes an average constant wall # ux while the second approach accounts for axial pressure-dependent # ux. However, both models were useful only for low values of wall suction and so, were unable to predict performance accurately. From the experiments, it was concluded that wall suction has maximum e ! ect on the axial pressure drop at intermediate axial # owrates (1000 ( Re F ( 15 000).

texts.orig: Coker, Freeman and Fleming (1998) and Thorman and Hwang (1978) recognized the limitations of the Hagen } Poiseuille equation. Modi " cations to the pressure drop equation were made to account for " bre permeability and gas compressibility. Coker et al. (1998) derived the pressure drop equation from the steady-state, isothermal mechanical energy balance for gas in plug # ow through hollow " bre. However, in the context of their study, they have shown that the in # uences of gas compressibility and gas # ow through the side walls of the " bres on the lumen pressure pro " le are negligible, hence, pressure drop in the lumen can still be described by the Hagen } Poiseuille equation. Thorman and Hwang (1978) carried out a detailed investigation of the tube-side pressure loss. Several factors like radial and axial deformation, radial # ow, gas expansion and end e ! ects have been considered and incorporated into a re " ned pressure loss model. The axial pressure loss relationships for compressible # ow in an impermeable tube and incompressible # ow in a permeable tube were combined by means of a " rst-order perturbation technique to describe compressible # ow in a permeable tube. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The objective of this paper is to study pressure drop in the lumen of hollow " bre membranes when a compressible gas or a gas mixture is used. A pressure drop equation has been developed from the continuity equation and the momentum balance equations with the consideration of gas compressibility and " bre permeability. For the case of negligible permeation # ux, this more complex equation reduces to the Hagen } Poiseuille equation. Comparison of the results obtained from the two equations has been carried out and separation of a volatile organic vapour from N 2 stream has been used as an example to illustrate the importance of the pressure drop equation developed.
texts.text: The objective of this paper is to study pressure drop in the lumen of hollow " bre membranes when a compressible gas or a gas mixture is used. A pressure drop equation has been developed from the continuity equation and the momentum balance equations with the consideration of gas compressibility and " bre permeability. For the case of negligible permeation # ux, this more complex equation reduces to the Hagen } Poiseuille equation. Comparison of the results obtained from the two equations has been carried out and separation of a volatile organic vapour from N 2 stream has been used as an example to illustrate the importance of the pressure drop equation developed.

texts.orig: been carried out using the improved Hagen } Poiseuille equation developed in this study. Thorman and Hwang (1978) and Coker et al. (1998) analysed the pressure pro " les in lumen of hollow " bres with consideration of " bre permeability and gas compressibility. However, they concluded that the e ! ects of these two factors on the pressure pro " les are negligible. Hence, in this study, the conventional Hagen } Poiseuille equation has been used for extensive comparison with the pressure pro " le obtained from the improved model developed in this study. Published values of permeabilities for a composite membrane (Peinemann & Ohlrogge, 1994) were used in simulation. The geometric characteristics of the permeator used are listed in Table 1, while the operating conditions for the permeator and the permeabilities used for all the gas/vapour components involved are given in Table 2. Pure gas/vapour permeation and separation of a volatile organic vapour from N 2 stream have been used as examples to illustrate the importance of the pressure drop equation developed.

texts.orig: the improved Hagen } Poiseuille and Hagen } Poiseuille models for both shell- and lumen-feed were carried out. Simulation for the ideal case where there is no pressure drop in the " bre lumen was also performed. Besides studying the e ! ect of pressure pro " le on permeate purity, the e ! ect of membrane selectivity and feed mode on pressure pro " le, stage cut and permeate purity are also discussed. The organic vapours used are butane and n -pentane, which have selectivity values of 25 and 50, respectively (Peinemann & Ohlrogge, 1994). Besides a di ! erent selectivity, the other reason for selecting these two organic vapours is that their viscosity values are very close which allows a more accurate comparison of the theoretical results. As butane and n -pentane are volatile organic vapours, dew point calculations were performed so as to ensure that they do not condense out at the operating temperature and pressure employed. The feed stream has been assumed to consist of 5 or 10% of the organic vapour in balance of N 2 . The operating conditions for the permeator and the permeability values used for all the components involved are given in Table 2.

This means that the driving force for the improved model is only slightly higher than that for the Hagen } Poiseuille model which explains the earlier observation of negligible di ! erence in purity values obtained for the two pressure models.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A new pressure drop equation, referred to as the improved Hagen } Poiseuille model has been developed. This pressure model takes into consideration the e ! ects of " bre permeability and gas compressibility but still retains the simplicity for computation. Comparison between the improved model and the existing Hagen } Poiseuille equation for the pure gas permeation of some permanent gases and organic vapours is carried out. According to theoretical predictions, the Hagen } Poiseuille equation can be used to represent the pressure pro " le for permanent gases while the improved model is more suitable for predicting the pressure pro " le in the lumen for organic vapours. In addition, changes in physical and geometrical parameters such as increasing membrane permeability and decreasing " bre radius would result in a greater deviation in pressure drop and stage cut obtained from the two pressure models. Simulations of gas/vapour separation using an example of separation of a volatile organic vapour from N 2 were also performed for the two pressure models with the consideration of shellor lumen-feed operation.
